data.title: Oxford Industries Continues To Struggle But Trades At More Than 15x Earnings
data.description:
Oxford Industries, Inc. (NYSE:OXM) reported Q2 '25 results, with sales trends worsening from Q1 '25 in a much more important quarter (seasonally). The company commented on a potential improvement in sales into Q3, but with margins down big.

In this context, the company has added debt to pay dividends and buy back shares. I do not agree with this capital allocation policy, given the uncertain context, where net cash (or lower net debt) plays a strategic role.

Based on company guidance, the name trades at 15/17x FY25 earnings. I believe this valuation is very high considering the lack of growth, margin deleverage, risky capital allocation, and the economic context. I maintain a Hold on OXM.

Q2 '25 results

Worsening in a key quarter: Q1 and Q2 are the company's key quarters, in terms of seasonality, given the warm-weather skew of the brands. Of course, Q4 and the Holidays help at the end of the year, but H1 '25 is more important than H2 '25.

In this context, the company's sales worsened in Q2, with sales down 4% versus 1% in Q1. Retail comps were down 5%, same as last quarter, but this time wholesale moved down 6%, versus a positive 4% last quarter.

Brand trend unchanged: By brand, the situation is unchanged from Q1 '25, and one could even say from FY24.

Lilly Pulitzer is the strongest performer, maintaining a small positive LSD figure. Still, this is decelerating from double-digit growth in Q1.

Tommy Bahama is the biggest driver of underperformance because it is the largest brand. It was down big, HSD in comps. This is probably the worst point in the results.

Johnny Was continues to underperform, with low double-digit declines. This is a trend since the brand was acquired, and during the call, management said that the brand is under turnaround efforts with big changes in many areas. JW currently serves as a very worrying sign of bad brand management, as it has never surpassed the sales from the time when the brand was acquired.

Finally, the Emerging Brands segment (which includes about four brands) was up, although I do not understand if there are comparable figures there because of the acquisition of Rogers.

Margins collapsed: The most negative results were not in the topline, but at the bottom line. Margins moved down significantly, losing 50% of operating income YoY ($28 million vs $57 million) and with margins halved (7% vs. 13.5%).

This has been a double whammy between more promotionality (needed to attract traffic in the current economy, and because of assortment and brand problems), and higher CoGS from tariffs (these two leading to 160bps of gross margin declines), and the fact that sales were down despite adding a lot of stores (almost 70 net new stores in FY24 and FY25), meaning SG&A grew in dollars and that it deleveraged 500bps.

Forward may not be better

The company provided one very positive point looking ahead. It guided sales to be LSD down to flat at the aggregate level in Q3, but with stores comping positively, including TB flat.

However, a few things are to be noted. First, last year Q3 was down big, almost 6%, with HSD negative comps in brick and mortar retail, because of hurricane season and the U.S. election. So it is comping an easy quarter. Second, the quarter is the smallest seasonally by almost a 25% versus any other quarter in the year. That means, lower profitability in general from leverage. And finally, and most importantly, margins are expected to remain very challenged, down 300bps on gross margins, and with SG&A up MSD (meaning deleverage versus flat or down LSD aggregate sales).

That is, the only positive development will be the positive comps, with the rest pointing towards a relatively bad Q3.

For Q4, the situation is in flux, with the impact of tariffs (aggregate $25/35 million net of supplier discounts and unmitigated costs) hitting mostly in H2 '25, and with a response from the company that will greatly depend on the demand situation, which, as we have seen, is not great at the moment.

The company maintained guidance in general, with revenues down a little, but margins and EPS flat.

Risky capital allocation

In a context where margins are down big, where profitability at the operating level is much lower, and where important brands are down HSD, I would expect the company to act conservatively in terms of the balance sheet and capital allocation.

This is not what OXM has done this year. YTD, the company has paid dividends of $21 million and repurchased shares for $55 million. Although most of this can be covered with the operating profitability of H1 '25 alone ($66 million in operating profits, so about $50 million in NOPAT), the strategy still led to an increase in debt, to $81 million versus $31 million in Q4 '24. That is, the company has reduced equity by increasing debt.

That is not a conservative way of managing the balance sheet. If the dividend was not expected to be cut, then the company could have saved on the repurchases. We should remember that in the same year, the company has increased CAPEX substantially (to $55 million in H1 '25 and expected to net $120 million in FY25), so that its cash requirements were already high.

Of course, OXM is not currently in a bad financial position, given that $80 million in debt is less than a year of profitability. But still, I believe the strategy is not what I would prefer.

Valuation remains exaggerated

I have maintained a negative view on OXM's valuation since March 2024 (stock down 50%+ since). The correction in the price seems to have come about via the decrease in expected earnings ($2.8/3.2 EPS in guidance for FY25 vs $6.7 in FY24), but not in the multiple associated with those earnings.

The company has some good brands, with pricing power, but still, it has significant top-line and margin challenges, and is not executing well. In addition, the context is fairly negative, both because of tariffs directly impacting margins, and because of consumers becoming more cautious with spending.

Today, OXM trades at a price of $47 per share, or about 15/17x FY25 guided earnings. I do not believe that a company posting comps down 5%, where operating margins are halving, and guiding for even more challenges, deserves to trade at such valuations.

In my opinion, OXM would start to be attractive at half that multiple, meaning either one believes earnings will double soon, or the price has to be much lower.

For that reason, I maintain a Hold rating on OXM.

data.title: Starbucks Doesn't Excite Me Like It Used To (Downgrade)
data.description:
In March 2024, I wrote a bullish article regarding Starbucks (NASDAQ:SBUX), the massive global coffee chain that millions of consumers across the globe line up for every day. Even though I'm not the biggest fan of it from a consumer perspective, I do like their hot chocolate. And, back in early last year, I found myself bullish about the name because of the expectation of continued growth and how the stock was valued. Since then, however, we have seen some weakness. Even though revenue continues to expand, this is really only because of an increase in location count and the firm's decision to continue raising prices for its customers. At the same time, profitability has been weakening as of late. And as a result, shares have gotten more expensive.

Management is working on making improvements here. But with how the stock has become valued on a forward basis, the picture is not all that exciting to me at the moment. If the firm can return to the level of profitability that it saw even last year, I would be more optimistic. But with significant issues weighing on it and the way the stock is valued, I believe that downgrading it to a "Hold" makes the most sense.

A tough time for Starbucks

If you were to look at fundamental data covering just the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years, things should actually look relatively decent for Starbucks. During this time, for instance, revenue for the company expanded from $32.25 billion to $36.18 billion. For much of the company's life, it has benefited from a rise in comparable store sales. This has historically been fueled by rising transactions and higher prices per transaction. Sure enough, in 2022, comparable sales jumped 8%, with the average ticket price growing 5% while the number of transactions grew 3%. In 2023, we saw another 8% rise, fueled by a 3% increase in the number of transactions and a 5% rise in tickets. But then, in 2024, the picture changed. During that year, comparable sales actually dropped by 2%. Even though management said that the firm saw a 2% benefit from higher average tickets, transactions dropped by 4%. It appears as though this weakness can be chalked up to a combination of macroeconomic worries and, to some extent, competitive pressures.

Even though there has been some weakness when it comes to comparable sales, it is important to keep in mind that this is only one piece of the pie. And it is the piece that applies to the company-operated locations that the firm has. Over the last few years, company-operated stores grew from 18,253 to 21,018. But on top of this, the company has also seen growth in the number of stores that are licensed, with that number expanding from 17,458 to 19,181. In total, this means growth in the number of locations and operations from 35,711 to 40,199. For such a large business with a global reach, this is impressive.

As comparable sales started declining, the company's profitability hit a snag. After seeing net income jump from $3.28 billion in 2022 to $4.12 billion in 2023, there was a drop to $3.76 billion last year. Most of this weakness came from its international operations, with a decline in revenue from $7.49 billion to $7.34 billion, pushing operating profits down from $1.23 billion to $1.06 billion. A lot of this pain was the result of a rise in store operating expenses from $2.76 billion to $2.82 billion. This weighed significantly on the bottom line for its international operations. And according to management, the overall decline in profits for that portion of the company was attributable to higher promotional activity, higher wages, and higher benefits. It is disappointing that even higher promotional activities did not succeed in boosting sales and that they had a negative impact on the bottom line of such a significant magnitude.

There were other areas where the company saw weakness as well. Its Channel Development unit, for instance, saw revenue contract from $1.89 billion to $1.77 billion. This was instrumental in pushing operating profits down from $967.6 million to $925.9 million. It does appear, however, that this bottom-line decline was because of a $91.3 million gain on the sale of its Seattle’s Best Coffee brand that the company completed in 2023. Without this, operating profits here would have actually risen. When it comes to its North American operations, meanwhile, revenue grew, but profits still fell, contracting from $5.50 billion to $5.36 billion. Higher investments in employee wages and benefits, the cost of deleveraging caused by weaker comparable sales, and higher promotional activities all worked together to cause this.

Other profitability metrics for the company showed mixed results from 2023 to 2024. Operating cash flow, for instance, grew from $6.01 billion to $6.10 billion. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a virtual flat line at $7.14 billion. On the other hand, EBITDA for the business fell from $6.98 billion to $6.72 billion.

This year, things are looking even more challenging. For the first nine months of 2025, revenue came in at $27.62 billion. That represents an increase of 1.9% compared to the $27.10 billion the company reported the previous year. This growth came even though comparable sales dropped 2%, with a 4% decline in transactions more than offsetting a 2% rise in tickets. Yes, the number of locations in operation grew from 39,477 in the first half of last year to 41,097 at the same time this year. So that certainly helped.

Unfortunately, bottom-line results for the company have proven to be challenging. Net income has plummeted from $2.85 billion to $1.72 billion. Some of this was because of $137 million that the company spent on restructuring initiatives. But most of it involved higher costs, including a rise in store operating expenses from 42.1% of sales to 46.1%. Higher labor expenses, deleveraging associated with the decline in comparable sales, and higher marketing activities caused this pain. Unfortunately, all other profitability metrics for the company followed suit. Operating cash flow plummeted from $4.56 billion to $3.37 billion. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $5.48 billion to $4.59 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company contracted from $5.10 billion to $3.95 billion.

The good news is that management is making changes to improve operations in the long run. This includes uplifting at least 1,000 stores across North America by the end of next year. It also includes the introduction of a new store prototype that will cost 30% less to build while simultaneously sunsetting its mobile order and pickup-only concepts next year. While the last of these items might seem odd, this should not be confused with the company getting rid of mobile orders and pickup-only orders. Rather, it is centered around the fewer than 100 locations that the firm has in operation, many of which are in office buildings and have little or no seating, that the firm will be getting rid of. This is because the firm wants to return to its roots of its coffee house identity. And, most likely, these activities are lower-margin in nature.

Management is also working on launching new menu innovations for next year. Examples that they have already shared with investors include protein cold foam, a new 1971 dark roast coffee, and more. Simultaneously, the company is also supposedly evaluating options regarding its operations in China. Some rumors suggest that it could sell that part of the business for $5 billion or more.

The company's plans also include its recently launched initiative called Green Apron Service, which came out in August of this year. This, according to the company, will involve improving customer service through how the company greets customers, offering customers glasses or mugs when appropriate, putting personalized messages on cups, and other things aimed at improving the feel of going there. Investing more in employees, changing their queue technology to fulfill cafe orders in under 4 minutes on a consistent basis, and more are all examples of this. This follows a broader transformation plan launched by the company in September of last year that management has called Back to Starbucks and that includes some of the items I've discussed already, as well as simplifying its menu by cutting around 30% of items on it, reintroducing the self-serve condiment bars and free refills, and cutting a large number of corporate roles to improve operating costs.

These are all positive things. But until we see some real progress on this front, I think it is too early to become terribly optimistic. If we project our results based on what we have seen so far for this year, the company should generate net profits during 2025 of $2.27 billion. Adjusted operating cash flow should be about $5.99 billion, while EBITDA should come in at around $5.21 billion. In the chart above, we can see how the company is valued using these estimates and using historical results from last year. These numbers aren't bad, but they aren't great. And for a firm that is dealing with some fundamental issues, I think it puts the company more in the fair value range than it does in the undervalued range.

Takeaway

Starbucks is going through an interesting time. I suppose that there is the potential for upside if things go right. But between the troubles the company has, how early it is in its turnaround process, and how the stock is valued, I just don't see a reason to get terribly optimistic. The company looks to be more or less fairly valued at this moment. And because of this, and despite the changes that management has made and will continue to make, I believe downgrading it to a "Hold" makes the most sense.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Energy Transfer, Vertical Aerospace, QuantumScape And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser-covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today we're looking at articles published between Sep. 5 and Sep. 11.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

Energy Transfer: Massive Distribution Yield And Discount

Dair Sansyzbayev | Strong Buy

My previous bullish thesis about Energy Transfer LP (NYSE:ET) did not age quite well because its -1.6% total return since May 28 is way worse compared to the broader equity market's. Nevertheless, I remain bullish and reiterate my "Strong Buy rating both for ET and for the company's Series I Fixed Rate Perpetual Preferred Units (NYSE:ET.PR.I) because its fundamentals remain ironclad. Financial performance is improving from the profitability perspective despite flat revenues, which is crucial to maintain ET's compelling distribution yields. ET is poised to benefit from the ongoing AI revolution as well. Last but not least, the stock is still extremely cheap.

Vertical Aerospace: Now An EVTOL Leader (Rating Double Upgrade)

Stephen Tobin | Buy

In November 2022, I first covered Vertical Aerospace (NYSE:EVTL), rating it a Sell. It was the first Sell article to be published on Seeking Alpha, and since then, 11 articles have been published, with 10 rating it a Sell or Strong Sell and one a Hold.

Today, I am moving to a bullish stance, a clearly contrarian viewpoint, and with the stock down 89% since my first article, I am running against the trend, being greedy when others are fearful, as the saying goes.

QuantumScape: Huge Progress Overlooked (Rating Upgrade)

Stone Fox Capital | Buy

Despite a lot of positive developments, QuantumScape Corporation (NYSE:QS) is actually trading much lower than it was a couple of months ago. The first live demonstration of an electric vehicle powered by QuantumScape solid-state batteries was a huge step forward towards a production launch. My investment thesis is more Bullish on the stock after the positive development of the last few months, combined with QuantumScape slipping back down to $8 for a much better technical setup.

Bristol-Myers Squibb: Growth Portfolio Surpasses Legacy, Driving Oncology And Immunology Leadership

The J Thesis | Strong Buy

Bristol-Myers Squibb (NYSE:BMY) is a global biopharmaceutical heavyweight company that has significantly underperformed the broader market over the past 5 years, with its stock price declining 23%.

The company is discovering and developing innovative medicines for a wide range of diseases in therapeutic areas like oncology, hematology, and cardiovascular conditions. With innovative focus on immuno-oncology, the company is shifting away from a declining legacy business and pivoting to a fast-growing pipeline which is designed to offset patent expiries of legacy products.

BMY trades at a forward P/E of 7.26, which is nearly 60% lower than the industry average of 18.09, indicating limited downside and potential for a rebound. Despite the revenue setback from legacy blockbusters, the company's growth portfolio sales increased 17% year-over-year, suggesting a turnaround may be near. I think the company presents an attractive opportunity for long-term investors while it trades at such undervalued levels.

That is why I think Bristol-Myers Squibb is a Strong Buy with a $54 price target over the next 12 months, indicating nearly 13% upside — and over 18% when taking into account the dividend yield of 5.23%. The company's legacy business is in decline, which justifies a major correction in its stock price, but I think it may be positioned for a long-term turnaround.

CommScope: Amphenol Deal Offers Fresh Start, But Questions Remain

Ian Bezek | Hold

I've been following electronic components manufacturer Amphenol (APH) for quite a few years now. I've always been impressed with that management team's ability to produce strong growth and elevated profitability out of what might, on the face of it, seem like a fairly mundane industry. And now, with the AI and data center-led boom, Amphenol has delivered absolutely sizzling results over the past couple of years.

So it was with interest that I noted Amphenol's latest acquisition target, CommScope's (NASDAQ:COMM) broadband connectivity and cable unit ("CCS"). On August 8th, Amphenol announced that it will be paying $10.5 billion for CCS. On August 22nd, Amphenol secured $4 billion in loan facilities, which should help enable this deal to close promptly.

This is an absolutely massive deal for CommScope. At one point earlier this year, CommScope had a market capitalization of less than $1 billion. And even after the rally post-Amphenol deal announcement, shares are still quoted at around a $3.5 billion market capitalization today. With Amphenol paying $10.5 billion for CommScope's largest business unit, does that leave more upside for COMM stock going forward?

Synopsys: Selloff Brings Shares Down, But Not Down Enough

Grassroots Trading | Hold

I last rated Synopsys, Inc. (NASDAQ:SNPS)a Hold. Valuation did most of the heavy lifting on that call with shares priced as if the company already had all its initiatives in the bag. This was while new leverage for the pending Ansys deal and policy risk around China still loomed. Yes, the China sales clearance improved the setup. Even so, at the time, I felt the multiple had already captured that good news and then some.

Since my rating, and prior to this morning’s gruesome reaction to its earnings (shares trading down around 23%) SNPS did manage to have a better pace than the broader market, rising about 11% versus around 4% for the S&P 500 (SPY).

My view is that demand for electronic design automation is strong and the Ansys deal should give Synopsys a helpful hand. But that upside is balanced out by a stock that’s still priced high, more debt on the books, and risks tied to China and chip foundries.

SPYT: 20% Yields And Limits Of Option "Income"

Trapping Value | Strong Sell

Bull markets can make you feel invulnerable. Tariffs? Recession? Highest valuations in history? No problem. This train has only one direction. Unfortunately, bull markets cannot bend the laws of math (though they can temporarily fool the law of gravity). The law in question here is that the higher the current valuation gets, the worse it will get for future retirees. We will get more into that in another article, but for now, let's look at a fund that is using the current market madness to advertise like this.

Aim to achieve a target annual income of 20% in the S&P 500 using options, paid out monthly

Source: SPYT

The fund in question is Defiance S&P Income Target Fund (NYSEARCA:SPYT) and is yet another in a long line of option strategy funds that have been an unfortunate side-effect of the bull market. We go over the fundamentals and tell you why you should really stay away.

Solid Power: On The Right Path To Market Readiness

Tangerine Tan Capital | Buy

As I like to look at small companies that could potentially disrupt large industries, I came across Solid Power (NASDAQ:SLDP). And Solid Power is a company with huge potential but also risks, as it is not yet 100% certain that its technology will ultimately prevail.

But I like to build up positions in such small, interesting companies that are around 1% of my portfolio, because if the company doesn't succeed, it's a loss that doesn't really matter in the long run, but if the company is successful, I'm very happy to average up.

However, I will probably build up small positions in several players in the battery industry. Solid Power will also be one of them, as I find the technology interesting, as I think that Solid Power has competitive advantages and that they have a conservative balance sheet, and strong partnerships, and are on the right path to market readiness.

Brookfield: My Long-Term Anchor For My Portfolio

Jonah Crema | Buy

When I look for new companies to invest in, I start by picturing the world ten years from now; what will be in greater demand, which problems will need solving, and which companies will be essential to meeting those needs. From that framework of thought, I work backward to find companies positioned to thrive in that future.

Brookfield Corporation (NYSE:BN) (TSX:BN:CA) is one of them. The next decade will demand more real assets such as energy, critical infrastructure, and more capital to fund it all. Its expanding insurance and reinsurance operations take on long-dated liabilities such as annuities and pension risk transfers, creating a float of permanent capital. That float is deployed into the assets Brookfield knows best, while its asset management arm raises and earns fees from outside investors, creating a flywheel of capital generation and deployment. I invest in real companies I would be comfortable owning outright. Brookfield's large-scale, disciplined capital allocation and consistent shareholder returns make it a company I am confident holding for the long term. Based on my valuation below, I expect Brookfield to be able to return at least 20% a year over the next five years, and I rate the company a buy.

UiPath: Buy Cheaper Than The Management - Opportunistic Deep-Value Play

Juxtaposed Ideas | Buy

We previously covered UiPath Inc. (NYSE:PATH) in July 2025, discussing how the SaaS company had missed the first train for Agentic AI monetization, as observed in the decelerating growth hinted by the management's FY2026 guidance and the intensifying AI SaaS competition.

Even so, we reckoned that its market leadership in the RPA market remained compelling, significantly aided by the growing ARR and the rich cash flows. Combined with the cheap valuations and the renewed growth opportunities arising from the recently released Agentic Automation in April 2025, we had reiterated our Buy rating then.

Since then, PATH has underperformed at -7.6% against the wider market at +2.5% and its automation/ AI SaaS peers to large degrees, with the further correction to the Q3'24 bottom in the $11s/ April 2025 bottom in the $9s playing out as expected in our prior article.

This is especially since the monetization of Agentic Automation released in April 2025 has yet to flow into its FQ2'26 performance metrics, as observed in the still decelerating Annualized Recurring Revenues [ARR] growth at $1.72B (+1.7% QoQ/ +10.9% YoY) compared to a year ago at $1.55B (+3.3% QoQ/ +19.2% YoY).

On the other hand, we are already seeing early hints of improved cross-selling trends, as observed in PATH's stable Dollar-Based Net Retention Rate of 108% (inline QoQ/ -7 points YoY), compared to a quarter ago at 108% (-2 points QoQ/ -10 YoY) and a year ago at 115% (-3 points QoQ/ -7 YoY), along with the growing adj gross profit margins of 84% (inline QoQ/ +1 YoY/ -6 from FY2021 levels of 90%).

The same has been observed in its expanding operational scale at richer adj operating margins of 18.3% in H1'26 (+9.6 points YoY/ +22.3 from FY2021 levels of -4%), with it underscoring why the worst of the SaaS company's growth headwinds may already be here and brighter days may be ahead.

What's Going On With EPR Properties? I'm In Once Again

Cash Flow Venue | Buy

EPR Properties (NYSE:EPR) holds a special place in my heart. Not only is it one of the best-performing REITs in my portfolio (despite the recent pullback), but it's also the first business I had the pleasure of covering on Seeking Alpha.

Since the time I published this article (assigning EPR a "strong buy" rating), the business has provided over 34% total return, with 22% resulting from stock price change (nearly matching the broad S&P 500 index) and adding another 12 percentage points through EPR's highly attractive dividend payments. ...

However, something seems to have changed over the last couple of months, as EPR's stock price has fallen from over $61 per share to ~$53 per share. That's a double-digit correction.

Something had to change substantially for EPR to head south like that, right? Well, not necessarily, as EPR is well-known for being quite volatile, so let's investigate!

Enbridge: Buy This 5.6% Yield Midstream Company

Louis Gerard | Buy

Since my last analysis on Enbridge, the company has posted a strong total return of 8.01% on the back of strong quarterly earnings and a slight increase in distribution. I want to offer a fresh review on one of the most crucial energy companies on the market.

As a reminder, Enbridge (NYSE:ENB) (TSE:ENB) is a major Canadian midstream company that operates the longest and most complex crude and liquids transportation system, as in the Mainline, highlighting the prowess of the company. Recently, the company has been focusing on modernizing its operations as well as expanding into renewables. With the recent earnings and the recent stock appreciation, I believe that now is the right time to offer a refreshed view on the Canadian giant.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Royalty Pharma: Investor Day Upside
data.description:
Following Royalty Pharma plc’s (NASDAQ:RPRX) Investor Day, we return with our commentary on the company. We have reviewed the Q2 results and continue to recommend a Buy rating, supported by consistent execution and an upward revision of guidance. Nonetheless, the share price has retreated since our previous update (Fig. 1), although it still records a 42% gain year-to-date. The company guided us through the key questions raised by investors and the buy-side (Fig. 2). This exercise proved valuable in sharpening our view on future projections while also helping to frame downside risks.

As a reminder, the rationale for our Buy rating rests on 1) a valuation gap compared to other royalty companies (that usually trade above their respective sector), 2) the ability to secure long-term royalties with upside on free cash flow generation, 3) supportive capital allocation priorities, and 4) upside from streamlining the corporate structure.

Fig 1

Source: RP Investor Day ppt - Fig 2

Why are we still positive?

Taking advantage of the Q&A Investor Day, we highlight the key points reinforcing our investment thesis.

  1. Royalties remain an essential source of financing within the biopharma ecosystem, particularly as biotech IPO markets remain unsupportive; moreover, they provide capital without diluting existing shareholders. In our last update, we increased our 2025 portfolio receipts growth estimate to 9% with a turnover of $3.1 billion. That said, the company achieved a portfolio receipts CAGR of 10% between 2020 and 2025. Royalty Pharma indicated that its outlook exceeds consensus by more than 10%, projecting a CAGR of 11–14% for 2025–2030. Therefore, there is a mismatch compared to our internal projection. We will update our financial assumptions within the "Earnings Changes and Valuation" paragraph. Growth in RP’s portfolio is underpinned by a robust balance sheet and the addition of newly acquired royalties;
  2. A recurring concern is that Royalty Pharma operates a unique business model with very few directly comparable listed peers. The company addressed this by presenting an update slide on the price-earnings multiple. While we acknowledge Royalty Pharma’s role as a capital allocator, we find limited justification in comparing it to "Alternative asset managers" platforms such as Blackstone, KKR, or Apollo. Having said that, the company trades less than 10x earnings, implying a roughly 40% discount relative to the broader biopharma sector.
  3. We see justification for a higher trading multiple at RP given that 86% of its portfolio comprises already approved therapies (Fig. 5). This also highlights management’s ability to secure best-in-class products, including blockbuster solutions. This is clearly illustrated in Figure 6 (peak sales at transaction and current sales).

Fig 3

Fig 4

Fig 5

Fig 6

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Here at Lab, we consider the CEO’s Q&A particularly impactful, as this update should help the investor community gain a clearer understanding of Royalty Pharma. Although intangible, it was notable that the CEO dedicated time to endorsing his team, while much of the presentation was delivered by younger members. This is an encouraging signal for the company’s long-term ambitions. Going to the numbers, we anticipated a 9% growth, while the company set a top-line 11-14% portfolio growth CAGR. By backtracking from the 2030 EPS outlook (Fig. 7), the company effectively signaled an implied annual EPS growth rate of around 12%. The RP Management incorporation and the learner cost structure help this. So, looking ahead, in 2026, still with a cautious approach, we model a 10% EPS growth on our $4.28 2025 EPS estimates, arriving at $4.7. Our calculation includes 583 million shares outstanding and does not include the current buyback.

Continuing to apply a 10x P/E target, which is 40% below the broader biopharma sector (Fig. 4), we increased our target price from $42.8 to $47 per share, representing a 30% upside. The RP target P/E is also aligned with its 5-year historical average, so we implied no multiple re-rating (Fig. 8).

Fig 7

Fig 8

Risks

We have an extended coverage of RP downside risks, so we report 1) the Vertex franchise development, 2) lower than anticipated portfolio receipts growth, 3) higher interest rate given the royalties' new investments, 4) lower synergies from incorporating RP Management team, and 5) value destruction in new royalties deals. Vertex's potential arbitration will likely result in litigation and higher legal costs.

Conclusion

With a 30% upside, ongoing buyback, and M&A firepower, we remain buyers of Royalty Pharma. This Investor Day will provide a tailwind for the company. Improved understanding within the financial community should contribute to a potential multiple re-rating and more accurate financial forecasts.

data.title: Travere Therapeutics: Buy Rating On Back Of Filspari Expansion Towards FSGS
data.description:
I'm starting coverage on Travere Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:TVTX) with a Buy Rating on its stock because I believe it has massive potential when it comes to its drug sparsentan. The first reason is because this endothelin and angiotensin II receptor antagonist has been approved to slow kidney function decline in patients with immunoglobulin A nephropathy [IgAN] who are at risk of disease progression. It is not just the mere fact that the company has been able to receive FDA approval of this drug, but the notion that it is seeing a massive spike increase in sales of it. Consider that U.S. net product revenues for Filspari have reached $71.9 million, which ends up being a year-over-year increases of 165%.

Why do I believe it is important to go over this biotech now? Well, the growth in net product revenues for this drug are good, but consider that there is potential for a major expansion opportunity. In essence, the company had submitted and the FDA had accepted a supplemental New Drug Application [sNDA] submission of Filspari [sparsentan] to treat patients with a rare kidney disorder known as focal segmental glomerulosclerosis [FSGS]. A Prescription Drug User Fee Act [PDUFA] date of January 13, 2026 was established in order to review by whether or not it should be approved to treat these patients. Here is the kicker, when the sNDA was first accepted, it was noted that the FDA was planning to hold an advisory committee meeting.

However, there recently was a huge update provided by Travere. It was notified by this U.S agency itself that such an advisory committee meeting would no longer be needed. The beauty of this company is that it holds the potential to have the first approved drug for the treatment of patients with FSGS. This doesn't even include the potential of its enzyme replacement therapy [ERT] Pegtibatinase [TVT-058], which is being developed to target patients with classical homocystinuria [HCU] in a phase 3 study to be known as Harmony. A late-stage trial of this caliber to target this group of patients is expected to start in 2026.

sNDA Provides Opportunity To Continue Massive Revenue Growth Of Filspari

As I noted above, Travere Therapeutics has already received FDA approval of its drug Filspari for the treatment of patients with immunoglobulin A nephropathy. Sales of this drug have been coming along very well and I will be going over such numbers in the "Financials" portion of this article. However, what I want to focus on specifically is the development of Filspari for the treatment of patients with a rare kidney disorder known as focal segmental glomerulosclerosis. That's because the company was not only able to submit the sNDA of this drug for the targeting of these patients, but the FDA has already established a specific PDUFA date.

The reason for the sNDA of Filspari being filed with the FDA is because of positive results from two studies that were completed. One was the phase 2 DUET study and the other one was the phase 3 DUPLEX trial. Before going over the data from either of these studies, plus any catalysts to come about from this specific program, it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis [FSGS] is a type of rare kidney disorder characterized as the formation of scar tissue in the glomeruli [small structures that filter out waste in the kidney]. What's the problem if this happens? With scarring coming about, along with inflammation, kidney functions are severely impacted. If not properly treated, then it could lead to kidney failure. Two such treatment options that might be possible would be dialysis or kidney transplant. There are several symptoms that these patients have to deal with, which could be one of the following:

  • Edema [swelling] in certain parts of the body like ankles or legs
  • Unexplained weight gain
  • Proteinuria [protein in the urine].

What makes it devastating is that it can arise with several causes. There is a genetic variation that is passed down from parents. Along with the formation of it due to toxic drug substances/medicines or from no known cause at all. The global focal segmental glomerulosclerosis market is expected to reach $51.72 billion by 2032. This is a large market opportunity and with the company potentially being on the cusp to provide superior proteinuria reduction compared to current 1st-line irbesartan, this is a good target for it.

With the symptoms I noted above, there is one in particular that relates to the efficacy endpoints established in both the phase 2 DUET and phase 3 DUPLEX studies. In both of these studies Travere was able to show that Filspari was able to reduce proteinuria by a considerable amount, especially compared to that of irbesartan. This particular drug is first-line standard of care [SOC] for the treatment of these patients with FSGS. Consider that this was developed with the mechanism of action [MOA] of blocking the angiotensin II receptor pathway specifically to reduce proteinuria seen in these patients. Where does this leave Filspari? Its mode of action is not only to act as an angiotensin II receptor blocker, but it also targets endothelin A receptor. The hope is that with this drug being able to block both of these receptor pathways, it is able to reduce proteinuria in a superior fashion compared to that of irbesartan. Travere's drug Filspari achieved such superiority compared to that of irbesartan in terms of proteinuria reduction in both children and adults in the DUET and DUPLEX studies.

The beauty of the company is that it ran the phase 3 DUPLEX study and the caliber of this goes beyond that of only showing that its drug Filspari was able to do well in terms of proteinuria reduction for the treatment of patients with focal segmental glomerulosclerosis [FSGS]. Why is that? Well, that's because it was the largest interventional study achieved showing that this drug was able to perform in a superior fashion compared to that than maximally dosed active comparator drug irbesartan. A total of 371 patients with primary and genetic FSGS were enrolled into this DUPLEX study and randomized 1:1 to receive either of the following doses:

  • 400 mg/day of FALSPARI titrated in week 2 and beyond to 800 mg/day as tolerated.
  • 150 mg/day of irbesartan titrated in week 2 and beyond to 300 mg/day as tolerated.

In terms of proteinuria in FSGS, this is where its drug shined well in. Consider that a pre-specified interim analysis of FSGS partial remission of proteinuria [FPRE] was achieved in a statistically significant manner of drug versus placebo. This endpoint over a 36-week period was shown to be achieved in 42% of patients given Filspari, compared to only 26% of those given active comparator irbesartan. The efficacy endpoint not achieved, though, was that of eGFR slope from day 1 to week 108.

The thing is that there is a major catalyst relating to this specific program and this would be the PDUFA target action date of January 13, 2026. This is the date by which the FDA will have by where it will decide whether or not Filspari should be approve for the treatment of patients with FSGS. A positive development for this program is that the company was notified that an advisory committee meeting is no longer necessary.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Travere Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $319.5 million as of June 30, 2025. A big reason on why it has the chat that it does is because of a public offering it enacted in November of 2024 to raise cash. That is it had an underwritten public offering of 9 million shares at an offering price of $134.7 million. The truth is that this company doesn't have a lot of cash on hand. Considering that it has a PDUFA target action date rapidly approaching, it will likely need to raise additional cash in the next several months.

This is based on what it notes in its very own 10-Q SEC filing, which is that it believes it only has enough cash on hand for a cash runway to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months. It does have a financial instrument in place if it chooses to utilize it. This would be with respect to an at-the-market equity offering agreement. Such an ATM agreement was established with Jefferies LLC, whereby it could offer and sell, from time to time up to $100 million of its common stock. To date no shares have been sold under this agreement, but this is an option that it has in place if it needs to raise cash immediately over the next several months.

The cash burn per quarter is approximately $125.6 million. This is broken down into $49.4 million in R&D expenses and then $76.2 million in SG&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Travere Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the upcoming PDUFA target action date of January 13, 2026. Even though Filspari has been approved to treat patients with IgAN, there is no assurance that it will be approved under the sNDA to treat patients with FSGS. In addition, while the drug was shown to do well in terms of proteinuria reduction compared to that of current SOC irbesartan, it did not meet the endpoint of eGFR total slope from Day 1 to week 108. Thus, the risk here is that there is no assurance that proteinuria reduction being achieved alone is going to enough to warrant U.S. marketing approval of Filspari for these FSGS patients.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the massive revenue spike seen in the most recent second quarter of 2025. It was revealed that net product revenues of Filspari reached $71.9 million during this most recent quarter, which ended up being a year-over-year revenue gain of 165%. The risk here is that upon the release of the next set of earnings from the company, there is no guarantee that the revenue to be generated will be similar to or superior to that which had already been reported. In essence, there is no assurance that the percentage growth rate in revenues is going to be this high.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that this company finds itself in. The reason why I state that is because of what it had noted in terms of cash projection runway. As I stated above, it believes that it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months. The risk here is that it may need to dilute existing shareholders in order to raise a considerable amount of cash to both fund its pipeline and to run its clinical studies. There is no assurance that it will be able to raise cash on considerable terms or to raise cash through its ATM agreement that it has in place.

Conclusion

Travere Therapeutics is in good shape for the long-term because it has already been able to receive U.S. marketing approval of its drug Filspari for the treatment of patients with IgAN. Where a milestone or catalyst opportunity exists is in terms of the upcoming expected FDA review of this drug for the treatment of patients with FSGS. The reason for such a review being possible is because of the sNDA that the company filed with the FDA. Not only was the sNDA of Filspari accepted by this U.S. agency but a PDUFA target action date of January 13, 2026 was set. The importance of this is that if the FDA approves this drug, it would be the first kind ever for the treatment of these patients with FSGS. Even if the FDA doesn't approve Filspari for FSGS immediately, I believe that not all would be lost.

First of all, it honestly depends upon the reason for the rejection. If it one minor CMC issue or something that can be fixed without requiring another large trial, then it will be easy to recover from such an issue. Secondly, the drug is already performing well in terms of revenue just on the IgAN indication alone. Why do I make such a claim? The reason why is because U.S. net product sales of Filspari grew by 165% year-over-year to $71.9 million during Q2 of 2025. If such a revenue growth trend continues then it will be good regardless of what happens with the upcoming PDUFA review date of Filspari for FSGS. With massive growth observed from Filspari in treating patients with IgAN, plus the potential to expand the market for this specific drug to target that of FSGS patients, I believe that it is important to set a Buy rating on this stock.

data.title: The Fed Won't Fix This
data.description:
All eyes will be on the Federal Reserve next week. The current consensus is overwhelmingly looking for a 25 bps reduction in the Fed Funds rate at the conclusion of the FOMC meeting.

Any possibility of a 50 bps cut probably went up in smoke after the hotter-than-expected August CPI reading earlier this week.

But not to worry, Morgan Stanley now projects the Federal Reserve will cut rates by 25 bps at each of their next four FOMC meetings. Unfortunately, the announcement of the rate cut at the end of the Fed's meeting next week feels like the classic 'buy the rumor, sell the news' type moment.

The markets have continued to rally despite a horrid series of jobs reports and downward job revisions over the past several weeks. Equities are priced at extreme levels using a variety of traditional valuation metrics like the 'Buffett Indicator' or market cap to GDP ratio. Stocks are also trading very dearly, looking at S&P 500 price-to-sales ratios, Shiller P/E, or NASDAQ price-to-sales ratios.

As importantly, the credit markets have also front-run this upcoming reduction in the Fed Funds rate. The yield on the 10-Year Treasury is down below 4.1% after being as high as 4.8% earlier in the year. Average mortgage rates have also come down to their lowest levels since last October.

In addition, a 25 bps, 50 bps, or even 100 bps in rate cuts in the coming quarters are only going to be of marginal help to the U.S. economy, although they should marginally lower debt service on the massive federal debt, which has blown through $37 trillion and continues to march higher at an alarming rate.

Lower mortgage rates will only marginally help what has become an increasingly toxic housing sector. For the first time since records were kept, the average cost of a new home was below the average price of an existing home in both June (6.5% discount) and July (4% discount). Of note, this does not capture the large incentives (mortgage buy-downs, help with closing costs, free upgrades, etc.) home builders are needing to employ to move inventory. Lennar (LEN) had just over $50,000 per home in incentives in Q2 with an average sales price of $389,000. Mortgage rates coming down to 5.5% to 6%, if that happens, will do little to revise the housing sector, although it could marginally lower the incentive costs for home builders.

Then we have commercial real estate, or CRE. As I just noted, the fundamentals for both the multifamily and office sectors continue to deteriorate. These two charts of CRE show that approximately $3.5 trillion of debt obligations will need to be refinanced at higher rates in coming quarters and years. A few interest rate reductions are not going to meaningfully improve market dynamics here, especially if the economy heads into recession in 2026.

Nor will slightly lower interest rates help consumers in a measurable way. The job market, as noted, is deteriorating. This is starting to impact consumer confidence. Consumer sentiment readings just fell from 58.2 to 55.4, and under the 58 consensus. This trend is likely to continue as long as the jobs market remains weak. We still also don't know what the impact of the AI revolution will be in the coming quarters. I recently penned a piece exploring whether AI will trigger a White-Collar Recession.

A slight decrease in interest costs will do little to help the situation with student loans, where delinquency rates are surging after payments have recently resumed after nearly a four-year taxpayer-financed hiatus. Over 40 million Americans have student loan balances. At the current and rising delinquency rate, millions will soon face wage garnishments and confiscation of any tax refunds in 2026.

A 25 bps point reduction in interest rates will also do little on the credit card front with a record $1.21 trillion in balances. And credit card delinquency rates are surging. Serious delinquent accounts reach seven percent in Q1, just one percent below that of the peak in the Great Financial Crisis.

A downtick in rates will also be of marginal benefit to auto loans, which now total a record $1.66 trillion. In 2025, the average new vehicle price has risen to nearly $50,000. Almost 20% of buyers are locked into $1,000 monthly payments, and one in five new auto loans in 2025 has seven-year terms. Auto repossessions have already hit their highest levels since 2009. Subprime auto loan delinquencies have moved over five percent, above their 2008 peak.

In summary, next week's financial headlines will be intently focused on actions and commentary at the conclusion of the FOMC meeting. It is highly likely that investors will see the first cut to the Fed Funds rate since September of last year. However, a 25 bps cut or even a series of them will not help the struggling jobs market. In addition, they will only be of marginal assistance in the consumer and real estate markets. Finally, the FOMC announcement is unlikely to power the next leg of a rally in a market that is clearly overbought, as the central bank can't fix what currently ails the U.S. economy.

data.title: INTF: International ETF With An Edge Over The Benchmark
data.description:
INTF strategy

iShares International Equity Factor ETF (NYSEARCA:INTF) was launched on 04/28/2015 and tracks the STOXX International Equity Factor Index. INTF has 458 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 2.67% and an expense ratio of 0.16%. Distributions are paid semi-annually.

As described in the prospectus by iShares, the methodology starts from the STOXX Global 1800 ex USA index, which measures the performance of the largest companies of the developed equity market excluding the U.S.

Stocks are selected and weighted using an optimization process based on five factors: momentum, quality, value, low volatility and size. The momentum score is calculated from price momentum, earnings momentum and earnings announcement drift. The quality score is calculated from profitability, share dilution, accruals, changes in net operating assets and ESG factors. The value score is calculated from book value-to-price, dividend yield, earnings yield and cash flow yield.

The portfolio is rebalanced quarterly with maximum limits of 10% in any constituent, 22.5% in aggregate in those weighing more than 4.5%, and some constraints on country and sector exposure. The turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was 22%. This article will use as a benchmark an international index excluding the U.S. and Canada, represented by iShares MSCI EAFE ETF (EFA).

The INTF portfolio

The portfolio has about 75% of asset value in large and mega-cap companies, with a focus on Japan (23.9%) and significant exposure in the UK (12.1%). Other countries are below 9%. The next chart lists the top 15 countries, representing 95.2% of assets. Unlike EFA, INTF includes Canada.

The portfolio has a focus on financials (26.7%), and significant exposure in industrials (18.3%) and consumer discretionary (12%). Other sectors are below 8%. Like the geographical allocation, the sector breakdown of INTF isn’t much different from the benchmark, although it moderately downplays healthcare and communication.

The portfolio is well-diversified and risks related to individual companies are low. Indeed the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent only 10.8% of asset value and the heaviest position weighs 1.93%.

INTF has the lowest expense ratio and comes in fourth position for total return and Sharpe ratio (a measure of risk adjusted performance) since December 2015. It is far behind the momentum fund, and close to EFV and QEFA. QEFA also has a shallower maximum drawdown and lower volatility, pointing to a safer long-term investment.

Takeaway

iShares International Equity Factor ETF is a multi-factor ex-U.S. ETF involving momentum, quality, value and low volatility. INTF is diversified in 400+ holdings, with a tilt to Japan, financials, and value style. INTF has outperformed the ex-U.S. benchmark EFA since its inception, especially over the last 12 months, but some competitors have a better track record. IDMO shows a much high return, but as a momentum fund it may be risky in market downturns. Despite higher fees, QEFA looks slightly superior to INTF on several points: return, dividend, dividend growth, volatility and drawdown. Nonetheless, INTF is a reasonable choice for investors seeking international diversification.

data.title: Amazon: Forward Inventory Buying Creates Uncertainties
data.description:
AMZN stock: FQ2 inventory in focus

I last covered Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) stock on July 25 with an article titled " Amazon FQ2 Earnings Preview: Why I Disagree With Wall Street.” The article served as an earnings preview for its FQ2 and rated the stock as a Hold. Since then, the company has released its actual earnings report (ER) for FQ2 on July 31. As one of the most popular tickers on the Seeking Alpha platform, the ER was quickly reviewed by many SA authors. Hence, in the remainder of this article, I will focus on an aspect that has not been discussed thus far to my knowledge – the issue of forward inventory buying.

To prime more detailed discussion, the chart below shows its balance sheet as of the end of FQ2 2025. As seen, Amazon's inventory levels have shown a notable upward trend over the past 2 quarters. Its inventory stood at $34.2 billion by the end of 2024. The company saw a moderate increase to $35.8 billion in Q1 of 2025, followed by a more significant jump to $40.8 billion in Q2 2025, a peak level since 2022. Next, I will argue why this surge could be due to forward inventory buying and why it could create near-term uncertainties.

AMZN stock: pros and cons of forward inventory buying

It is only natural for a company’s inventory to expand as its sales grow. However, Amazon's recent trend in inventory management suggests a potential of forward buying strategy. The strategy is the practice of purchasing and stocking up on goods in quantities greater than what is needed for immediate sales demand as reflected in the Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO) shown below. As seen, AMZN’s DIO in the past 2 quarters reveals that the company's inventory is sitting in its warehouses for longer periods than its historical average. As of FQ2 2025, the company’s quarterly DIO has risen to 43.30 days from about 32 days only in the end of 2024. The current reading is also notably above its historical average of 39.85 days.

I certainly see good reasons for the company to employ the forward buying strategy at this juncture. With the tariff/trade disputes, the strategy helps to protect AMZN against future price volatility and supply chain disruptions, providing stability in its inventory and costs.

However, the strategy also creates substantial downside risks in the meantime. A record inventory heightens the risk of obsolescence for AMZN, which can result in unsold inventory and/or obsolete goods that have to be sold at deep discounts. A heightened inventory also increases costs as it requires more warehouse space, higher costs for storage, insurance, and handling. As more capital is tied up in inventory and costs rise, AMZN's cash flow and financial flexibility can be strained, which is precisely the situation in my view per its Q2 ER as detailed next.

AMZN stock: free cash flow pressure

Amazon's Free Cash Flow (FCF) has been under pressure in recent quarters. As an example, the following ER slide shows that its FCF has been in decline since 2024. From a high of approximately $52,973 million in Q2 2024, it fell to $18,184 million by Q2 2025. This represents a significant year-over-year decrease of about 2/3.

Such a deteriorating in its FCF can slow down the pace of AMZN’s innovation and expansion. In particular, it can make it harder for AMZN to maintain its competitive edge and achieve its long-term growth goals. To compound the issues, the market segments that AMZN focuses on are all highly dynamics and require substantial growth CAPEX investments, such as its AWS (Amazon Web Service). As you can see from the second chart below, AMZN has been outspending its close competitors in recent years in CAPEX, and I expect CAPEX to further accelerate in 2025 and beyond as the AI race intensifies.

AMZN stock: other risks and final thoughts

Of course, there are plenty of upside catalysts afoot too judging by the updates in the ER. To start, AWS continues to exceed expectations and grow at rapid pace with quarterly run rates between 17%-19% in recent quarters. Its Internal Trainium2 chips are also progressing well and offer customers significant performance capabilities at lower cost. Finally, shortly after the ER, the company also announced the expansion of its delivery of same-day perishable grocery (see detailed quoted below):

Customers in more than 1,000 cities and towns now have access to fresh perishable groceries with free Same-Day Delivery. Amazon plans to expand this capability to over 2,300 areas across the U.S. by the end of 2025. Thousands of fresh grocery items are now available alongside millions of everyday Amazon.com products—all delivered within hours.

U.S. Grocery was an immense market and online penetration is quite low currently. As such, it could become a key growth area for AMZN in the years to come given its advanced delivery model and specialized temperature-controlled fulfillment network tailed specifically for this purpose.

In terms of valuation, Amazon's current FY1 P/E ratio stood at approximately 35x. This is moderately elevated in my view either by absolute or relative comparison. For example, when compared with the rest of mag 7 stocks, its P/E ratio is towards the higher end of the spectrum. It is notably higher than Alphabet's (GOOGL) P/E of 24x and Meta's (META) 27x and is only lower than Tesla (TSLA) and Nvidia (NVDA). When adjusted for growth, the P/E-to-Growth (PEG) ratio is also quite elevated. As seen, its FWD PEG ratio is close to 2x, not only far above the idea threshold of 1x but also above the ratios for several Mag 7 stocks. However, given the issues mentioned above (record inventory, CAPEX, etc.), the valuation risks are more pronounced when assessed in P/FCF ratios as you can see from the last screenshot below.

All told, this AMZN follow-up is motivated by its FQ2 ER, in particular the potential issues of forward inventory buying. This strategy offers some benefits amid the tariff/trade disputes, but I also see plenty of downside risks. The ER certainly described plenty of upside catalysts too, but these catalysts are counterbalanced by the uncertainties in my view – at least for the short term. As such, I see no obvious bias in its return/risk ratio and reiterate my hold rating.

data.title: SCHX: Large Caps Should Continue To Perform
data.description:
The Schwab U.S. Large-Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHX) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund designed to track the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Total Stock Market Index, spreading risk across 750 of the largest U.S. companies. SCHX is a low-cost strategy with a 3 bps expense ratio and pays out a modest distribution rate of $0.29/share, yielding 1.11% on a trailing 12-month basis.

About Schwab U.S. Large-Cap ETF

SCHX was launched on November 3, 2009, by Charles Schwab Asset Management on the NYSE Arca Exchange. The strategy was designed to track the performance of the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Total Stock Market Index, a portfolio consisting of 750 of the largest U.S. equities. SCHX has over $59 billion in assets under management [AUM] with an average of $283 million in share value changing hands on a daily basis, providing investors with substantial liquidity with minimal spread risk.

SCHX is a passively managed ETF, meaning the strategy is designed to mirror the holdings and weights of the underlying index. SCHX has a beta of 1x when compared to the benchmark Index, meaning the strategy should be expected to provide full exposure net of fees. This means that investors will be responsible for tactical positioning in the strategy, as SCHX may not take a defensive position during periods of market stress.

SCHX is heavily weighted to the top 10 holdings, making up 36% of the total portfolio weight. By comparison, the bottom 10 holdings make up a mere 2.5% of the total portfolio weight. The top holdings are largely weighted towards the Magnificent 7 and mega-tech names, including Nvidia (NVDA) at 7.33%, Microsoft (MSFT) at 6.30%, Apple (AAPL) at 5.74%, Amazon (AMZN) at 3.69%, Broadcom (AVGO) at 3%, Meta Platforms at 2.8%, and Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) at 4.26%.

The top holdings largely mirror the top 10 holdings in the S&P 500 (SPX) and Nasdaq-100 (NDX), potentially making SCHX an alternative to popular index funds like the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) or the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ). Comparing performance, SCHX has generally tracked in line with these two indices during short increments. In the long run, QQQ has substantially outperformed SHCX; SCHX performed in line with SPY over long holding periods. At a high level, underweighting of the mega-cap technology stocks may have led to QQQ’s outperformance relative to SCHX in the last year.

Looking at industry weights, SCHX will likely be most influenced by the technology sector, with a weight of 34%.

In terms of growth and performance, I believe SCHX may be set for continued growth resulting from the increased investment in AI infrastructure. One of the most recent signals came from Oracle Corporation’s (ORCL) robust Q1’26 earnings results, portraying a significant runway for AI infrastructure investment in the coming years. As part of the growth cycle, Broadcom recently announced a partnership with OpenAI to develop custom AI accelerators. OpenAI is also a major customer of Oracle as part of its $500 billion Stargate project, scaling large data centers to power its AI development. Though the partnership between Broadcom and OpenAI may impact Nvidia’s position as a GPU supplier for data centers, I do not believe Nvidia’s growth trajectory will be significantly impacted, given the growth across the broader market.

Comparing peer strategies, SCHX is one of the lowest-cost ETFs for exposure to large-cap stocks, with the iShares Morningstar U.S. Equity ETF (ILCB) offering a competitive fee of 3 bps. Comparing performance, QQQ has far outperformed peer large-cap strategies over time. Net of the major indices, SCHX has outperformed peer funds across most historical periods, making this an appealing portfolio to consider for diversified large-cap exposure.

Risks Related to SCHX

SCHX is a passively managed ETF, making investors responsible for tactical position and portfolio management. The strategy currently exhibits an average 27.59 price/earnings ratio across the portfolio, potentially suggesting substantial premium risk going forward.

Final Thoughts

SCHX can be an appealing component for a diversified portfolio strategy, providing low-cost exposure to 750 large-cap equities. Given the comparable performance to the Nasdaq-100, investors should consider the level of equity concentration and exposure that suits their investment needs. With the expectation of the broader markets continuing to perform, I am recommending SCHX with a "Buy" rating as part of a broader investment strategy.

Using market capitalization as a basis for one’s portfolio strategy, I believe investors should consider rotating more heavily into small- & mid-caps while maintaining some exposure to large-caps.

data.title: Cava: The Inevitable Slowdown Hammer
data.description:
Cava (NYSE:CAVA) was the hottest topic in the restaurant sector since its IPO, with the stock reaching valuation heights never seen in the sector.

The idea of buying "The Next Chipotle" (CMG) pushed analysts to do some interesting acrobatics to justify Cava's valuation. At the end of the day, as I've warned, the momentum had to stop, and Cava's industry-leading growth had to come down to earth at some point, as the initial halo effect begins to fade.

Investors are now looking at a stock in a 58% drawdown that still trades at a lofty valuation, and wondering what they should do. I argue that Cava is still too pricey here, and encourage patience.

Background

Cava went public in a June 2023 IPO with an actual public price of around $40, at a market cap of about $4.7 billion. I wrote at the time that Cava has the potential to become America's next big fast-casual chain, and yet, I thought the valuation was highly risky, reflecting the market's eagerness to participate in what could very well be "The Next Chipotle."

Little did I know this eagerness would go so far as to push the stock to almost triple in less than two years. Still, I continued to argue that valuation is very far ahead of fundamentals, and that chasing stocks that trade entirely on momentum is one of the worst risk/reward trades investors tend to do.

Ultimately, after this extraordinary run, investors who held since IPO are now underperforming the market, after a near-60% drawdown from the 2024 peak.

A selloff that started due to risk-taking and value normalization was then exacerbated by disappointing Q2 numbers and a guidance cut.

Reality Check In Second-Quarter Results

Results in the second quarter missed expectations, with revenue coming 3% below estimates, at $278 million. EPS did beat, but at this point, it isn't a main metric investors look at.

Revenue growth of 20.3% Y/Y was primarily driven by new openings, with operating weeks up 17.6% Y/Y. Same-restaurant sales growth was 2.1%, the lowest level since Cava went public, as traffic was flat for the first time since the brand's inception.

Cava achieved restaurant-margin levels that are considered exceptional for the sector, which is quite impressive on such a small footprint, but it's both a gift and a curse. On one hand, it reflects strong unit economics. On the other hand, there's no major margin expansion story to lean on, at the restaurant level, as Cava is already at the high end of the industry.

The weaker-than-expected quarter led management to cut its outlook for the year, now expecting same-restaurant growth in the range of 4%-6%, down from 6%-8%.

Meanwhile, they are turning to new sauces, drinks, and a recently announced Chick Shawarma protein in hopes of driving frequency and protecting growth.

Overall, this was precisely the type of quarter I was expecting would send shares into a downward spiral; it just came a little earlier than I expected. Results were fine, but very disappointing against lofty expectations.

Cava Is No Longer At The Top Of The Industry

In my last article covering Cava, I said that investors are making a mistake by assuming its remarkable same-store growth levels are sustainable. I specifically said that Cava's extraordinary numbers raise long-term questions as they are reflective of a "New Player" halo, rather than structural tailwinds.

It turns out that my prediction was correct, as Cava went down to No. 9 last quarter, the worst level since its IPO except for Q1-24, which lapped unusually tough comps.

This time, although comps were indeed tougher than the previous quarter, it seems that the slowdown is much more fundamental and can be attributed to normalization, as well as market share gains from value-oriented players.

It shouldn't be surprising that companies like Taco Bell (YUM), McDonald's (MCD), and Domino's (DPZ) are winning over the consumer with their value offerings.

Notably, so far in 2025, the "value players" are significantly outperforming the "quality players" like Chipotle and Cava. It also didn't help that the latter group used to trade at far richer valuations on the premise of market share gains, which have now stopped, or at least been suspended.

Valuation & The Chipotle Dream

At $64 a share, Cava still trades at unprecedented valuations in the sector, at 116 times forward earnings, and 95 times next year's estimates. On sales, Cava is at a place we have seen occasionally, but for companies that are much more profitable and at a more predictable stage in their growth journey.

For instance, Chipotle has traded above 5 times sales occasionally since 2020. It was generating double the margin, it had more than 10x the number of stores, and it had already proven it can maintain high-teens growth at a large scale. Also worth noting that shares didn't perform so well from those valuation peaks.

In investing, too often investors justify bad near-term decisions with the excuse that they are long-term investors. Buying shares with significant intrinsic risk just because they could perform well in the long term isn't rewarded. In fact, it's one of the biggest drivers of huge losses for investors.

When a company like Cava trades on the dream of becoming the next Chipotle, fulfilling which is more than a decade away, investors can rest assured they'll have plenty of opportunities to buy the stock at a better risk/reward set-up.

Such a setup isn't fully a matter of price. It's also a matter of certainty, predictability, and stability. Waiting to see where Cava settles after its halo effect fades was, and still is, in my mind, the highest expected-value decision.

Conclusion

The slowdown hammer finally came down on Cava, as the company is now facing a challenging yet important stage of normalization.

I fully expect Cava to come out strong from this stretch, as I view their unique menu and strong management as highly likely to succeed in the long term.

I still believe Cava will grow to thousands of locations in the United States, and yet, even after a near-60% slowdown, I don't like the upside I'm getting from investing at the current valuation.

Cava still trades at unprecedented valuation levels for the restaurant sector, and until I get an attractive entry point, I'm willing to risk significant near-term losses for a reward that's far into the future.

As such, I reiterate a "Hold" rating for Cava.
